---
title: "Measurement, Indexing & Reliability"
subtitle: "Module 1 | PAF 516 — Community Analytics"
author: "Arizona State University"
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: beaver
    fonttheme: professionalfonts
    slide-level: 2
    aspectratio: 169
    toc: false
    section-titles: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \definecolor{asuMaroon}{HTML}{8C1D40}
        \definecolor{asuGold}{HTML}{FFC627}
        \setbeamercolor{palette primary}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{palette secondary}{bg=asuGold,fg=black}
        \setbeamercolor{palette tertiary}{bg=asuMaroon!80,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{title}{fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{block title}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{alerted text}{fg=asuMaroon}
        \setbeamercolor{section title}{fg=white}
        \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
        \setbeamerfont{frametitle}{size=\large,series=\bfseries}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Part 1: What Does the Poverty Rate Measure?

## Harlem and Chinatown

Consider two Manhattan neighborhoods --- both with **40--80\% poverty rates**:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Harlem**

- 40--80\% poverty
- Mostly US-born residents
- High rates of inter-generational poverty
- Deep, persistent economic disadvantage
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Chinatown**

- 40--80\% poverty
- Many recent immigrants
- Few financial assets but strong social capital
- Children have high upward mobility
:::

::::

\vspace{0.3cm}

**Same poverty rate. Very different communities.** What is the poverty rate actually measuring?

## The Poverty Rate Hides a Lot

Two communities, both with a **25\% poverty rate**:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{p{6cm}p{6cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Community A} & \textbf{Community B} \\
\midrule
1\% enter poverty each year & 20\% enter poverty each year \\
1\% exit poverty each year & 20\% exit poverty each year \\
\textbf{24\% chronic poverty} & \textbf{5\% chronic poverty} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The **snapshot rate is identical** --- but the dynamics are completely different.

- Community A: deep, persistent poverty with very little turnover
- Community B: high turnover, most people cycle through quickly

A single variable cannot distinguish these fundamentally different experiences.

## What Does the Poverty Rate Actually Measure?

The federal poverty measure uses income thresholds that vary by family size. But "poverty" could mean many things:

- Lack of money?
- Lack of economic opportunity?
- Limited access to healthcare?
- Lack of education?
- Lack of mobility?
- Position in a social hierarchy?

::: {.callout-important}
Is a college student on a fixed budget "poor"? The poverty rate says yes. A single indicator cannot capture the full dimensionality of economic hardship.
:::

## Designing a Better Measure

> If you could define a new federal measure of poverty, how would you improve it?

**Dimensions to consider:**

1. **Financial capital** --- income, assets, debt
2. **Human capital** --- education, skills, health
3. **Social capital** --- networks, community ties, institutional access
4. **Physical health** --- chronic conditions, healthcare access
5. **Public goods** --- if you have good parks, free libraries, and public transit, do you need as much money?

**The insight:** A meaningful measure of neighborhood quality requires **multiple indicators** combined into a **composite index**. This is what measurement theory teaches us to do.

# Part 2: Measurement Theory

## The Core Challenge

> "Many variables studied by psychologists are straightforward and simple to measure... Other variables --- perhaps the majority --- are not so straightforward."

In community analytics, we care about things like **economic hardship**, **social vulnerability**, and **neighborhood quality** --- but none of these can be directly measured.

These are **latent constructs**: abstract concepts that must be inferred from observable indicators.

**This module teaches you to:**

- Define what you are measuring (the construct)
- Select observable indicators (census variables)
- Combine them into a reliable composite index
- Evaluate whether the index is internally consistent

## Module Learning Objectives

After completing this module, you will be able to:

1. Define a **construct** and distinguish it from directly measurable variables
2. Explain why multiple indicators are combined into a composite index
3. Apply **z-score standardization** before combining variables measured on different scales
4. Calculate and interpret **Cronbach's alpha** as a measure of internal consistency
5. Use `tidycensus` to retrieve census variables and `psych::alpha()` to assess reliability
6. Visualize inter-item correlations using `corrplot`

## Constructs vs. Indicators

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{3.5cm}p{5cm}p{5.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Concept} & \textbf{Definition} & \textbf{Example} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Construct} & Abstract concept that cannot be directly observed & Economic hardship, social vulnerability \\
\textbf{Indicator} & Observable variable reflecting one aspect of the construct & Poverty rate, unemployment rate \\
\textbf{Composite Index} & Single score combining multiple indicators & Economic Hardship Index (5 variables) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

**Why not use a single variable?** No single census variable fully captures "economic hardship." Poverty rate misses the working poor; unemployment misses discouraged workers. Multiple indicators **triangulate** the construct.

## Measurement Levels

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{2.5cm}p{4.5cm}p{3.5cm}p{3.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Level} & \textbf{Properties} & \textbf{Example} & \textbf{Valid Operations} \\
\midrule
Nominal & Categories, no order & County name, race & Count, mode \\
Ordinal & Ranked categories & Income quintile & Median, rank \\
Interval & Equal intervals, no true zero & Temperature (\textdegree F) & Mean, std.\ dev. \\
Ratio & Equal intervals + true zero & Income (\$), poverty rate & All arithmetic \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Census variables used in the Economic Hardship Index are **ratio-level** --- they have meaningful zeros and proportional relationships. This matters because z-score standardization requires at least interval-level data.

# Part 3: Building Composite Indices

## Why Combine Variables?

Three reasons to build a composite index rather than analyzing variables individually:

**1. Dimensionality Reduction**

- Summarize many variables into a single, mappable score
- Simplifies visualization and spatial comparison

**2. Construct Representation**

- No single variable fully captures a latent construct
- Multiple indicators triangulate the underlying concept

**3. Noise Reduction**

- Random measurement error in individual items averages out
- The composite is more reliable than any single component

## Measurement Theory: Signal and Noise

Each item (variable) can be decomposed:

$$\text{item} = X + e$$

Where $X$ is the true score and $e$ is measurement error.

When we combine items into an index:

$$\text{index} = T + e \quad \text{where } T = X_1 + X_2 + X_3$$

The reliability of the index is the **signal-to-noise ratio**:

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Var}(T)}{\text{Var}(T + e)}$$

As we add more items that measure the same construct, the signal grows faster than the noise. This is why composite indices are more reliable than single variables.

## Z-Score Standardization

Before combining variables on different scales, we standardize each to z-scores:

$$z = \frac{x - \bar{x}}{s_x}$$

This puts all variables on the same scale: **mean = 0, standard deviation = 1**.

**Why this matters:**

- Poverty rate is measured as a percentage (0--100)
- Median household income is measured in dollars (0--200,000+)
- Without standardization, income would dominate the index simply due to its larger numeric scale

After z-scoring, each variable contributes equally --- differences reflect actual variation, not differences in measurement units.

## The Economic Hardship Index

Five census variables, z-scored and summed:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{5.5cm}p{4cm}p{4.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Variable} & \textbf{ACS Table} & \textbf{Direction} \\
\midrule
Poverty rate & B17001 & Higher = more hardship \\
Unemployment rate & B23025 & Higher = more hardship \\
Median household income & B19013 & \textit{Reverse-coded} (lower = more) \\
\% No high school diploma & B15003 & Higher = more hardship \\
\% Receiving public assistance & B19058 & Higher = more hardship \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

::: {.callout-warning}
Median household income must be **reverse-coded** before summing: multiply the z-score by $-1$ so that lower income = higher hardship, consistent with the other four variables.
:::

## Real-World Composite Indices

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{4cm}p{3cm}p{3cm}p{4cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Index} & \textbf{Variables} & \textbf{Method} & \textbf{Use} \\
\midrule
CDC Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) & 16 census vars & Percentile rank & Emergency preparedness \\
Townsend Deprivation Index & 4 census vars & Z-score sum & Health disparities (UK) \\
PAF 516 Economic Hardship Index & 5 census vars & Z-score sum & Course lab framework \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

All three share the same logic: select indicators of a latent construct, standardize, and combine.

> Townsend, Phillimore \& Beattie (1987) pioneered the z-score approach using unemployment, overcrowding, non-home ownership, and non-car ownership.

# Part 4: Reliability --- Cronbach's Alpha

## What Is Cronbach's Alpha?

> *Cronbach, L. J. (1951). Coefficient alpha and the internal structure of tests. Psychometrika, 16, 297--334.*

Alpha is the most widely used measure of **internal consistency** --- it answers: **do the items in our index hang together?**

If all items measure the same underlying construct, they should be **positively correlated** with each other.

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Var}(T)}{\text{Var}(T + e)}$$

- $\alpha = 1.0$: all variance is signal (perfect reliability)
- $\alpha = 0.0$: all variance is noise (no reliability)
- In practice, $\alpha \geq 0.70$ is the standard threshold

## Interpreting Alpha

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{p{4cm}p{4cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Alpha Range} & \textbf{Interpretation} \\
\midrule
$\alpha > 0.90$ & Excellent \\
$0.80 < \alpha \leq 0.90$ & Good \\
$0.70 < \alpha \leq 0.80$ & Acceptable \\
$0.60 < \alpha \leq 0.70$ & Questionable \\
$\alpha \leq 0.60$ & Poor --- reconsider items \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

**In Lab 1:** You will compute alpha for the 5-variable Economic Hardship Index using `psych::alpha()`. An alpha above 0.70 confirms the five census variables measure a coherent underlying construct.

::: {.callout-tip}
The `psych::alpha()` output includes an "alpha if item dropped" column --- this tells you whether removing any single variable would improve reliability.
:::

## When Alpha Is Low

If $\alpha < 0.70$, your items may not measure the same construct. Diagnostic steps:

1. **Check the correlation matrix** --- are some items uncorrelated or negatively correlated with the rest?
2. **Look at "alpha if item dropped"** --- does removing one variable substantially increase alpha?
3. **Reconsider the construct** --- maybe the items measure two different things (e.g., economic hardship vs. social isolation)
4. **Check for reverse-coding errors** --- a variable pointing the wrong direction will reduce alpha

::: {.callout-warning}
A high alpha does not prove construct validity --- it only shows internal consistency. Items could consistently measure the *wrong* thing.
:::

## Correlation Matrix: corrplot

The correlation matrix visualizes **inter-item relationships** before computing alpha.

**What to look for:**

- All items should show **positive correlations** with each other (after reverse-coding)
- Correlations in the **0.30--0.80 range** are ideal --- too low suggests items measure different things; too high suggests redundancy
- A single item with near-zero correlations is a candidate for removal

In Lab 1, you will use `corrplot::corrplot()` to visualize the 5×5 correlation matrix of your index variables. Strong positive correlations across all five variables confirm they tap the same latent construct.

# Part 5: Multi-Scale Mapping

## Why Map at Multiple Scales?

The same index mapped at three geographic levels tells different stories:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{3cm}p{3cm}p{4cm}p{4cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Geography} & \textbf{Approx.\ N} & \textbf{What It Reveals} & \textbf{What It Hides} \\
\midrule
US County & \textasciitilde3,100 & National patterns, regional clusters & Within-county variation \\
Census Tract & \textasciitilde1,700 (AZ) & Neighborhood gradients, urban/suburban & Some MOE at small tracts \\
Block Group & \textasciitilde900 (Maricopa) & Block-level variation, hyper-local & Highest margins of error \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

::: {.callout-important}
This is a preview of the **Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP)**, which Module 2 covers in depth. The key insight: your conclusions depend on the scale you choose.
:::

## Lab 1 Workflow

Lab 1 walks you through the complete index-building process:

1. **Pull census data** with `tidycensus::get_acs()` at county, tract, and block group levels
2. **Calculate component rates** from raw count variables
3. **Z-score standardize** each variable (reverse-code income)
4. **Sum z-scores** into the Economic Hardship Index
5. **Assess reliability** with `psych::alpha()` and `corrplot`
6. **Map the index** at all three geographic scales using `ggplot2` + `sf`

::: {.callout-tip}
The tutorial file is fully executable --- knit it first, then run chunk by chunk to understand each step.
:::

## Module 1 Summary

**Key takeaways:**

- **Constructs require multiple indicators.** No single census variable captures economic hardship, social vulnerability, or neighborhood quality.
- **Z-score standardization is essential.** Variables on different scales must be standardized before combining.
- **Reverse-code opposing variables.** Income points the opposite direction from poverty --- multiply its z-score by $-1$.
- **Cronbach's alpha validates the index.** $\alpha \geq 0.70$ confirms internal consistency. Use `psych::alpha()` and check "alpha if item dropped."
- **Scale matters.** The same index mapped at county, tract, and block group reveals different patterns and supports different policy conclusions.

**Coming up in Module 2:** Classification \& Spatial Scale --- how classification method and geographic unit interact to shape map interpretation (MAUP).
